summary:
The Congress of Berlin of 1878 was a diplomatic conference of the major European powers that revised the terms of the Treaty of San Stefano, which had ended the [[event:3646|Russo-Turkish War of 1877–78]] and threatened to give Russia disproportionate gains in the Balkans. Hosted by Bismarck in his self-declared role as 'honest broker,' the Congress represented the Concert of Europe's last significant exercise in collective great-power management of the Eastern Question.

The Treaty of San Stefano had created a large, Russian-aligned Bulgaria stretching to the Aegean Sea — which Britain and Austria-Hungary viewed as a dangerous extension of Russian influence toward the Mediterranean and the Bosphorus. Britain had dispatched a fleet to the Sea of Marmara, and Austria-Hungary had mobilized reserves; war between the powers seemed possible. Bismarck's offer to host a congress at Berlin provided a face-saving mechanism.

The Congress's revisions significantly curtailed the Treaty of San Stefano's terms. Bulgaria was divided and reduced in size, with much of Macedonia returned to Ottoman rule. Austria-Hungary received the right to occupy and administer Bosnia-Herzegovina. Britain gained Cyprus from the Ottomans in a secret side-deal. Russia gained Bessarabia and territories in the Caucasus. Romania, Serbia, and Montenegro were recognized as fully independent states.

The Congress satisfied no one completely and planted seeds of future conflicts. Russia felt humiliated by the revision of its military gains. The pan-Slavic movements in the Balkans were outraged that Macedonia remained under Ottoman rule. Serbia was antagonized by Austria-Hungary's acquisition of Bosnia. The settlement's instabilities contributed directly to the Balkan Wars of 1912–13 and ultimately to the First World War, whose immediate trigger — Austria-Hungary's confrontation with Serbia — descended directly from the antagonisms the Congress of Berlin had failed to resolve.
